abstract: Artificial intelligence research increasingly depends on prolonged cycles of reproduction, debugging, and iterative refinement to achieve State-Of-The-Art (SOTA) performance, creating a growing need for systems that can accelerate the full pipeline of empirical model optimization. In this work, we introduce AutoSOTA, an end-to-end automated research system that advances the latest SOTA models published in top-tier AI papers to reproducible and empirically improved new SOTA models. We formulate this problem through three tightly coupled stages: resource preparation and goal setting; experiment evaluation; and reflection and ideation. To tackle this problem, AutoSOTA adopts a multi-agent architecture with eight specialized agents that collaboratively ground papers to code and dependencies, initialize and repair execution environments, track long-horizon experiments, generate and schedule optimization ideas, and supervise validity to avoid spurious gains. We evaluate AutoSOTA on recent research papers collected from eight top-tier AI conferences under filters for code availability and execution cost. Across these papers, AutoSOTA achieves strong end-to-end performance in both automated replication and subsequent optimization. Specifically, it successfully discovers 105 new SOTA models that surpass the original reported methods, averaging approximately five hours per paper. Case studies spanning LLM, NLP, computer vision, time series, and optimization further show that the system can move beyond routine hyperparameter tuning to identify architectural innovation, algorithmic redesigns, and workflow-level improvements. These results suggest that end-to-end research automation can serve not only as a performance optimizer, but also as a new form of research infrastructure that reduces repetitive experimental burden and helps redirect human attention toward higher-level scientific creativity.

citations.fullCitation: [7] Alexander Novikov, Ngân V ˜ u, Marvin Eisenberger, Emilien Dupont, Po-Sen Huang, Adam Zsolt Wagner, Sergey Shirobokov, Borislav Kozlovskii, Francisco JR Ruiz, Abbas Mehrabian, et al. Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.13131, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is frequently contrasted with AutoSOTA to highlight differences in scope. While AlphaEvolve focuses on using evolutionary methods to mutate and improve single, bounded algorithms, AutoSOTA tackles the much larger problem of optimizing entire multi-file research repositories, positioning it as a more complete, end-to-end system.
citations.title: Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

citations.fullCitation: [6] Andrej Karpathy. Autoresearch. https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch, 2026. GitHub repository.
citations.justification: AutoSOTA is explicitly framed as an advancement over AutoResearch. The paper notes that AutoResearch is confined to a "sanitized sandbox" and sidesteps critical real-world challenges like environment setup and dependency resolution, which are core components of AutoSOTA's end-to-end workflow.
citations.title: Autoresearch

citations.fullCitation: [20] Chris Lu, Cong Lu, Robert Tjarko Lange, Jakob Foerster, Jeff Clune, and David Ha. The ai scien- tist: Towards fully automated open-ended scientific discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.06292, 2024.
citations.justification: This citation places AutoSOTA within the ambitious field of 'Autonomous AI Scientists.' It represents the broader vision of automating the entire scientific lifecycle, providing context for AutoSOTA's contribution as a specialized, fault-tolerant infrastructure focused on the crucial but difficult phase of SOTA model optimization and replication.
citations.title: The ai scientist: Towards fully automated open-ended scientific discovery

citations.fullCitation: [15] John Yang, Carlos E Jimenez, Alexander Wettig, Kilian Lieret, Shunyu Yao, Karthik Narasimhan, and Ofir Press. Swe-agent: Agent-computer interfaces enable automated software engineering. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 37:50528–50652, 2024.
citations.justification: SWE-agent is a key predecessor that demonstrated the viability of LLM agents in navigating and resolving issues within complex, multi-file software repositories. This foundational capability is essential for AutoSOTA's agents that perform code modification, debugging, and optimization, making it a critical reference for the system's underlying technology.
citations.title: Swe-agent: Agent-computer interfaces enable automated software engineering

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: AutoSOTA: An End-to-End Automated Research System for State-of-the-Art AI Model Discovery

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "AutoSOTA: An End-to-End Automated Research System for State-of-the-Art AI Model Discovery" was authored by a team primarily affiliated with Tsinghua University and Zhongguancun Academy.

The authors and their affiliations are:
*   Yu Li (Tsinghua University)
*   Chenyang Shao (Tsinghua University, Zhongguancun Academy)
*   Xinyang Liu (Tsinghua University)
*   Ruotong Zhao (Tsinghua University)
*   Peijie Liu (Tsinghua University)
*   Hongyuan Su (Tsinghua University, Zhongguancun Academy)
*   Zhibin Chen (Tsinghua University)
*   Qinglong Yang (Tsinghua University)
*   Anjie Xu (Zhongguancun Academy, Peking University)
*   Yi Fang (Zhongguancun Academy, University of Science and Technology of China)
*   Qingbin Zeng (Tsinghua University)
*   Tianxing Li (Tsinghua University)
*   Jingbo Xu (Tsinghua University)
*   Fengli Xu (Tsinghua University, Zhongguancun Academy)
*   Yong Li (Tsinghua University, Zhongguancun Academy)
*   Tie-Yan Liu (Zhongguancun Academy)

### 2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape

Artificial intelligence research is characterized by an emphasis on achieving State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance. This often necessitates extensive cycles of implementation, reproduction, debugging, evaluation, and iterative refinement. While conceptual novelty is valued, a method's impact is frequently determined by its empirical superiority over existing benchmarks. This process consumes significant human effort in translating theoretical insights into reproducible performance gains.

The presented work, AutoSOTA, situates itself within the evolving landscape of automated AI research, aiming to accelerate this empirical model optimization pipeline. It builds upon previous efforts such as OmniScientist, which explores fully autonomous scientific discovery. Existing approaches in this domain include:

*   **Automated Machine Learning (AutoML)**: Traditional AutoML systems, like those for hyperparameter optimization (HPO) and neural architecture search (NAS), have demonstrated value in optimizing predefined search spaces. However, these systems are generally limited to specific operations and typically do not facilitate the invention of novel algorithmic structures, synthesis of external knowledge, or debugging of complex software environments.
*   **LLM-Driven Algorithm Discovery and Code Optimization**: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have enabled systems to generate and optimize programmatic logic, navigate multi-file repositories, and resolve software engineering issues. Examples include systems that discover mathematical heuristics (FunSearch), optimize reward functions (Eureka), or iteratively mutate algorithmic code (AlphaEvolve). Open-source initiatives like AutoResearch aim to automate experimental loops. However, many of these approaches operate in sanitized sandbox environments, confine interventions to bounded code modules, and often bypass complexities of environment initialization, dependency resolution, and long-horizon debugging. They may also lack robust supervisory mechanisms to ensure methodological comparability and prevent spurious gains when modifying SOTA repositories.
*   **Autonomous AI Scientists**: More ambitious projects aim to automate the entire scientific research lifecycle, including idea generation, experimental execution, and manuscript drafting (e.g., AI Scientist, AI Scientist v2, AI Co-Scientist, DeepScientist, ResearchAgent). These systems often focus on macro-level ideation or manuscript generation, but may not provide the robust, long-horizon execution grounding required for reliably advancing highly competitive empirical baselines.

AutoSOTA addresses these gaps by proposing an end-to-end automated system that starts from unstructured research papers, establishes executable environments, reproduces reported baselines, and then performs valid, open-ended methodological reflection to discover empirically improved SOTA models. It aims to bridge unstructured literature with executable environments and high-level reflection within a single closed-loop framework, focusing specifically on the robust and fault-tolerant optimization of empirical AI models.

### 3. Key objectives and motivation

The primary objective of AutoSOTA is to develop an end-to-end automated research system capable of discovering new State-of-the-Art (SOTA) AI models. This involves transforming an unstructured research paper from a top-tier AI conference into an advanced, executable repository that empirically outperforms the original method. This overarching goal is motivated by the increasing reliance of AI research on extensive and often repetitive cycles of implementation, debugging, and refinement to achieve SOTA performance. This process consumes significant human resources that could otherwise be directed towards more original and disruptive scientific exploration.

To make this complex and open-ended task tractable, the work decomposes the global objective into three tightly coupled sub-problems:

1.  **Resource Preparation and Goal Setting**: The first sub-problem involves grounding a theoretical paper into a concrete experimental task. This requires interacting with the open web to identify and extract the foundational codebase, necessary datasets, base models, and precisely define the target performance metric that the system must ultimately exceed. Key challenges in this phase include the fragmented and implicitly coupled nature of academic resources, such as non-standardized repository links, dispersed dependencies, and the complex, multi-dimensional presentation of empirical results in papers, which makes it difficult to pinpoint a singular, scientifically meaningful performance benchmark.

2.  **Experiment Evaluation**: After resource acquisition and objective definition, the system must create a functional repository state whose empirical evaluation closely approximates the reported target metric. This phase converts static resources into a dynamic execution pipeline. The core difficulty lies in navigating environment configurations, implicit hardware dependencies, and deprecated library versions, often under conditions of partial observability. Academic code is frequently incomplete, suffering from missing utility scripts, hardcoded paths, or undocumented pre-processing steps. The system must diagnose these failures, synthesize missing logic from conceptual descriptions, and avoid pathological debugging loops.

3.  **Reflection and Ideation**: This phase aims to elevate the system beyond mere replication to autonomous scientific discovery. It involves uncovering genuine algorithmic improvements, exploring structural innovations, novel learning objectives, and optimized implementation strategies. A significant challenge here is intelligently balancing open-ended exploration against the high computational cost of real-world experimental trials. The system must iteratively reflect on historical execution trajectories, propose substantive modifications rather than superficial hyperparameter tuning, and ensure that all metric gains are methodologically valid, avoiding spurious results.

By addressing these interconnected challenges, AutoSOTA aims to alleviate the burden of repetitive experimental iteration in AI research and serve as a research infrastructure that amplifies human creativity by automating large-scale execution, reproduction, and reflection.

### 4. Methodology and approach

AutoSOTA employs a multi-agent architecture comprising eight specialized agents, structured to mimic human AI research workflows. These agents are organized into three core macro-modules: Resource Preparation and Goal Setting, Experiment Evaluation, and Reflection & Ideation. The Experiment Evaluation and Reflection & Ideation modules operate in a continuous iterative cycle.

#### 4.1. Resource Preparation and Goal Setting Module

This module bridges unstructured academic literature with actionable engineering constraints, involving two agents:

*   **AgentResource**: This agent is responsible for grounding research papers to executable environments. It performs a two-stage pipeline:
    *   **Paper-to-Repository Grounding and Curation**: It starts by collecting papers from eight top-tier AI conferences, normalizing their metadata, and parsing text. An LLM-based classifier identifies methodological papers, filtering out theoretical or review articles. For these candidates, it extracts GitHub links from PDFs and project pages, selects the most relevant official repository, and performs a shallow clone. A lightweight readiness assessment then estimates if the repository is actionable for reproduction.
    *   **External Resource Acquisition**: It addresses the common absence of heavyweight dependencies (datasets, pre-trained weights) within Git repositories. This involves:
        *   **Symbolic Resource Discovery**: Parsing repository structure and evaluation manifests to catalog external requirements, creating a Global Resource Registry with source URLs, resource taxonomies, and estimated file sizes.
        *   **Physical Acquisition and Orchestration**: Utilizing a Download Execution Agent that operates on the validated registry. It includes gated selection and filtering based on size, semantic storage into standardized directories (e.g., `datasets/`, `models/`), polymorphic retrieval modalities (e.g., `huggingface-cli`, `curl`, LLM-driven custom Python scripts), and concurrency with fault tolerance (paper-level parallelism, deduplication, breakpoint resumption).
*   **AgentObjective**: This agent constructs a structured evaluation space, or rubric, for large-scale experiment replication and optimization. It automatically maps the paper's macro research goal into a dense feedback library, addressing the scalability bottleneck of manual rubric construction.
    *   **Tree-Structured Recursive Decomposition**: It uses a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm driven by LLMs to decompose macro-replication objectives into granular, multi-dimensional binary (Pass/Fail) sub-tasks. A weight-driven termination mechanism ensures focus and efficiency.
    *   **Hierarchical Context Injection**: To provide evidentiary support for the rubric, it integrates knowledge from PDF Markdown, visual information (tables/figures) via VLM, and underlying engineering logic from the codebase. This occurs via a tiered, on-demand context delivery mechanism: shallow layers for macro-level info, intermediate layers for methodology and experimental details, and deep layers for fine-grained implementation logic.

#### 4.2. Experiment Evaluation Module

This module focuses on transforming noisy academic repositories into functional baselines and evaluating new algorithmic variants, distributing responsibility across three agents:

*   **AgentInit**: Responsible for workflow-grounded execution initialization, converting the paper-grounded task interface into an actionable execution plan and a runnable repository state. It maps paper, repository, resources, and rubric into an initial executable state ($R_0, E_0, \Pi_0$). It uses a preset workflow (input analysis, sub-task decomposition, environment construction, etc.) and a reusable skill library (e.g., `Repo2Run` for environment setup, Docker fallback, dependency installation, code editing for path repair, command recovery). It also performs protocol-preserving repository repair, synthesizing missing glue logic or repairing file-system assumptions without altering the scientific protocol.
*   **AgentMonitor**: An external supervisory agent that performs real-time state tracking and high-level intervention. It observes the execution trace ($T_{0:t}$) to infer the execution state ($s_t$) and generate supervisory actions ($a_{sup_t}$) like `continue`, `resume with guidance`, or `terminate`.
    *   **Execution Trace Monitoring and Deadlock Intervention**: It continuously parses the execution trace to detect failure patterns and dead-end trajectories (e.g., repeated environment edits without progress). It provides high-level corrective guidance, not low-level patches. It also acts as a global budget and safety controller, tracking time/compute limits and terminating unresponsive processes.
    *   **Persistent Context Management via External Memory**: It maintains a structured external memory (e.g., `code_analysis.md` for codebase structure, `idea_library.md` for optimization trajectory, `research_report.md` for external knowledge) to manage state and context over long iterative processes, bounding context usage and enabling coherent long-horizon reasoning.
*   **AgentFix**: A dedicated repair module for resolving runtime conflicts, turning failure handling into an explicit, reusable component. It maps observed failures ($\epsilon_t$) into validated repair actions ($\Delta_t$), leveraging a memory of historical failures and remedies ($M_{err}$). It emphasizes skill-augmented repair, consulting a structured skill repository for known error categories (e.g., package installation errors, CUDA toolchain mismatches) before attempting arbitrary modifications. It maintains reusable memory artifacts to record failures, attempted repairs, and outcomes, preventing cyclical reasoning.

#### 4.3. Reflection & Ideation Module

This module elevates the system to autonomous scientific discovery, involving three highly coupled agents:

*   **AgentIdeator**: Constructs a structured search space of admissible improvement hypotheses. It is framed as protocol-respecting structured hypothesis construction, providing a risk-aware hypothesis library and an audit record.
    *   It queries an external, research-capable model to generate a task-relevant prior report grounded in SOTA literature and empirical heuristics.
    *   It enforces a "red-line system" to filter proposals violating experimental integrity across four dimensions: Metric Alignment, Implementation Alignment, Constraint Alignment, and Lever Alignment.
    *   The output is a systematized hypothesis library, where each hypothesis is categorized by granularity (PARAM, CODE, ALGO) and risk level, with an admissibility predicate (`Adm(h)`) ensuring scientific validity.
*   **AgentScheduler**: The orchestration core for managing the complete lifecycle of a reproduced paper, from environment initialization to iterative code modification, evaluation, and result export.
    *   **Optimization Strategy**: Structured into four phases:
        *   **Phase 0 (Initialization & Baseline Measurement)**: Launches Docker, initializes version control, measures baseline metrics, and records them.
        *   **Phase 1 (Code & Paper Understanding)**: Explores repository structure, identifies algorithmic branches, maps reasoning pipeline, estimates runtime, and enumerates hard constraints.
        *   **Phase 2 (Idea Library Construction)**: Generates an initial `Idea Library` with annotated candidate optimization directions (type, priority, risk, assumptions), followed by a mandatory Red Line Audit to filter out invalid ideas.
        *   **Phase 3 (Iterative Optimization Loop)**: Executes a seven-step loop (reflection, idea selection, Git snapshot, coding, evaluation, debugging, result recording, idea update). It includes a "Leap Path" mechanism to prevent stagnation in low-dimensional parameter tuning, forcing structurally informed optimization ideas and granting a "Honeymoon Period" for their evaluation. An internal version control layer ensures atomic state transitions and rollback capabilities.
    *   **Resource Management**: Manages a global resource framework for multiple papers and GPUs, using a two-GPU unit as basic granularity. It maintains persistent tracking of scheduling state for recovery from crashes, and launches tasks in independent processes for long-term unattended operation.
*   **AgentSupervisor**: Enforces methodological validity and comparability, preventing invalid optimization behaviors through a "Red Line System." It ensures that any claimed performance gain is evaluated under identical experimental settings, consistent methodological constraints, and the same evaluation pipeline.
    *   The **Red Line System** defines six non-negotiable constraints (R1-R6) covering evaluation metric parameters, script integrity, algorithm output integrity, unfair metric trade-offs, dataset integrity, and data modification. An additional R7 requires paper-specific constraints to be explicitly identified.
    *   These constraints are enforced through a multi-layered supervision mechanism: in Phase 1 (identifying R7), after Phase 2 (Red Line Audit of all generated ideas), when the Leap Path is triggered in Phase 3 (explicit red line check for dynamic ideas), and via a global rule section in the main prompt, signaling their absolute precedence.

### 5. Main findings and results

AutoSOTA was evaluated on a curated benchmark of 105 method-driven research papers from eight top-tier AI conferences published within the past year. The papers were selected after a systematic, multi-stage filtering protocol that ensured methodological relevance, public code availability, repository readiness, and a manageable execution cost (single replication under 4 GPU hours).

The main findings are summarized as follows:

*   **Automated Replication and Optimization Performance**: AutoSOTA demonstrated performance in both automated replication and subsequent optimization. Across the 105 selected papers, the system successfully discovered new SOTA models that surpassed the original reported methods.
*   **Average Performance Improvement**: The system achieved an average performance improvement of approximately 10% over the original main methods reported in the papers. This indicates its capability to extract further performance augmentations even from already established top-tier research foundations.
*   **Computational Efficiency**: AutoSOTA required an average of approximately five hours of execution time per paper to achieve these improvements.
*   **Robustness Across Diverse Domains**: The system exhibited consistent robustness across multidisciplinary tasks, ranging from medical time-series analysis and protein binding energy prediction to various applications in LLMs, NLP, computer vision, and optimization. This indicates the generalizable potential of the framework.
*   **Comparison with Original Research Trends**: A comparison of average performance improvements across major research categories (CV, ML, NLP, Optimization, TimeSeries) showed that AutoSOTA maintained significant growth on a logarithmic scale, consistent with or even exceeding the trends observed in the original research data. Its results were characterized by lower variance and stronger consistency compared to traditional research data.

**Case Studies**: The paper presents several case studies illustrating the nature of AutoSOTA's optimizations, demonstrating that the framework can move beyond routine hyperparameter tuning to identify architectural innovations, algorithmic redesigns, and workflow-level improvements.

*   **LLM Research (FR-Spec)**: AutoSOTA identified a hidden error in the speculative decoding path of a language model, specifically an incorrect reuse of `tree_draft_ids` after verification, which corrupted embedding lookup and reduced throughput. Fixing this bug and making further runtime improvements (e.g., GPU-side terminal-token check, removing repeated CPU-GPU synchronization) led to a **13.44% improvement** in tokens per second.
*   **NLP Research (Comparing Moral Values)**: In a graph-based language analysis pipeline, AutoSOTA improved moral-value inference by changing how moral information is propagated through a word-association graph. This involved replacing raw edge-frequency counts with a `triple-log1p` transformation, tuning propagation strength, and redesigning the single-scale propagation rule into a multi-scale design. This resulted in an **8.59% relative gain** in `correlation_care`.
*   **Biology Research (Predicting mutational effects on protein binding)**: AutoSOTA improved protein binding affinity prediction by integrating orthogonal, physics-based signals. It identified and resolved a data-alignment bug in mutation string formats, enabling complete fusion of ML-based model predictions with classical physics-based FoldX predictions. An optimal blending ratio was found, yielding a **15.48% relative improvement** in per-interface Pearson correlation.
*   **CV Research (Reasoning as Representation)**: In an Image Quality Assessment task, AutoSOTA restructured representation bottlenecks in a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture. It redesigned the token fusion mechanism by introducing a three-way feature blending strategy, concatenating the global CLS token with average-pooled and max-pooled patch statistics, improving the Pearson Linear Correlation Coefficient (PLCC) by **2.68%**.
*   **Time Series Research (Neural MJD)**: For predicting volatile financial data, AutoSOTA performed deep, algorithm-level interventions. It dynamically scaled Monte Carlo sampling size, introduced antithetic variates to reduce gradient target variance, and reallocated loss weights. These refinements resulted in an approximate **19.0% improvement** in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) over the paper's baseline, demonstrating dynamic optimization and self-correction.
*   **Optimization Research (MoSES)**: In solving the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), AutoSOTA implemented a multi-layered intervention strategy. This included modifying the neural architecture's gating mechanism, aggressively scaling test-time symmetric augmentations (while managing memory), and injecting classical Operations Research heuristics for post-processing. This led to a **51.4% relative improvement** in the optimality gap.

These results collectively indicate that AutoSOTA is not limited to narrow coding tasks but can facilitate empirical advancements across heterogeneous AI research areas, demonstrating capabilities for problem diagnosis, architectural restructuring, and algorithmic refinement.

### 6. Significance and potential impact

The significance of AutoSOTA extends beyond the achievement of performance improvements on benchmarks. The system is presented as a novel form of research infrastructure for AI science, designed to mitigate the laborious and repetitive aspects of experimental iteration.

The potential impacts include:

*   **Acceleration of Scientific Discovery**: By automating the pipeline of reproduction, debugging, and iterative refinement, AutoSOTA can significantly accelerate the pace at which new SOTA models are discovered and validated. This could lead to a faster evolution of AI capabilities across various domains.
*   **Amplification of Human Creativity**: The system is intended to absorb the tedious, high-frequency cycle of experimental iteration, thereby freeing human researchers from low-level software engineering tasks. This redirection of human attention towards higher-level theoretical design, causal reasoning, and disruptive conceptual breakthroughs can potentially amplify scientific creativity and innovation.
*   **New Paradigm for Human-AI Collaboration**: AutoSOTA suggests a shift in the research paradigm towards tightly coupled human-AI collaboration. Automated agents handle large-scale execution, reproduction, and reflection, allowing human scientists to focus on conceptual innovation and address more fundamental scientific questions. This does not diminish the role of human researchers but redefines and sharpens it.
*   **Foundation for "AI Scientists"**: Within the broader vision of "AI Scientists" that aim to automate the entire scientific research lifecycle, AutoSOTA addresses a critical execution gap. While existing autonomous scientists may excel at macro-level ideation or manuscript generation, AutoSOTA provides the necessary empirical grounding, ensuring that AI-driven research is rigorously anchored in reproducible, SOTA-surpassing code execution.
*   **Enhanced Reproducibility and Reliability**: By providing an end-to-end automated system that bridges unstructured literature with executable environments, AutoSOTA inherently promotes reproducibility in AI research. The rigorous enforcement of a "Red Line System" ensures that all discovered algorithmic innovations are scientifically sound, comparable, and free from methodological shortcuts or spurious gains, thereby increasing the reliability of reported advancements.
*   **Cost-Effectiveness in Research**: The demonstrated computational efficiency (averaging five hours per paper for optimization) and fault-tolerant design contribute to a more cost-effective approach to large-scale empirical AI research, reducing resource waste associated with manual debugging and setup failures.

Future developments may include extending automated objective rubrics to more complex modalities like hardware-in-the-loop systems or robotics, and integrating AutoSOTA's execution engine with macro-ideation frameworks (e.g., OmniScientist). Such integrations could enable fully closed-loop systems where AI agents not only optimize existing SOTA methods but also proactively invent, empirically validate, and publish entirely new algorithmic paradigms.
overview:
## The Challenge of State-of-the-Art Discovery in AI Research

Artificial intelligence research is primarily driven by the pursuit of State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance. When a new method is proposed, its value is often judged by its ability to outperform existing benchmarks on standardized datasets. However, reaching this "frontier" is a labor-intensive process. Researchers must navigate a repetitive cycle of implementation, environment configuration, debugging, and iterative refinement. Translating a theoretical idea from a research paper into a working, high-performance repository often takes weeks or months of human effort.

This bottleneck is exacerbated by the "reproducibility crisis" in academic software. Research code is frequently released with missing dependencies, hardcoded file paths, or specific hardware requirements that are not fully documented. Furthermore, the search for improvements—such as architectural tweaks or hyperparameter tuning—requires hundreds of experimental trials, consuming significant computational and human resources.

![System Architecture Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05550v1/x2.png)
*Figure 1: The AutoSOTA multi-agent architecture, showing the interaction between Resource Preparation, Experiment Evaluation, and Reflection & Ideation modules.*

AutoSOTA is designed as an end-to-end automated research system to address these challenges. It functions as a research infrastructure that bridges the gap between unstructured scientific literature (PDFs) and executable, optimized code repositories. By automating the entire pipeline—from gathering datasets to reflecting on experimental results—the system seeks to accelerate the discovery of advanced AI models across diverse scientific domains.

## From Unstructured Literature to Actionable Engineering

The first major hurdle for any automated research system is "grounding": transforming a conceptual paper into a concrete experimental task. AutoSOTA handles this through its Resource Preparation and Goal Setting module, which utilizes two specialized agents: **AgentResource** and **AgentObjective**.

AgentResource is responsible for connecting a paper to the open web. It identifies official code repositories on platforms like GitHub, resolves external dependencies such as pre-trained weights or large datasets, and manages the physical acquisition of these assets. This involves navigating complex storage modalities, including Hugging Face repositories and custom download scripts.

Once the resources are gathered, AgentObjective constructs a structured evaluation "rubric." Rather than just looking for a single number, the system decomposes the research goal into a tree-structured library of sub-tasks. Using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm driven by Large Language Models (LLMs), it maps high-level goals into granular binary checks, such as environment configuration success, dataset preprocessing completion, and baseline metric matching. This ensures the system has a multi-dimensional feedback loop for the replication phase.

![Automated Rubric Construction](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05550v1/x4.png)
*Figure 2: The hierarchical process of building an experimental rubric from paper metadata and engineering logic.*

## Robust Execution and Self-Repair

Academic repositories are rarely "plug-and-play." They often suffer from deprecated library versions, missing utility scripts, or CUDA toolchain mismatches. To navigate this, AutoSOTA employs an Experiment Evaluation module comprising three agents: **AgentInit**, **AgentMonitor**, and **AgentFix**.

1.  **AgentInit**: This agent analyzes the repository and the rubric to create an initial execution plan. It maps the paper's requirements to an executable state $(R_0, E_0, \Pi_0)$.
2.  **AgentMonitor**: Acting as a high-level supervisor, it tracks the execution trace $T_{0:t}$ to infer the current state $s_t$. If a process stalls or enters a loop, the monitor generates supervisory actions $a_{sup_t}$ to terminate or redirect the effort.
3.  **AgentFix**: When a failure $\epsilon_t$ occurs, this agent diagnoses the error using a specialized "Skills Library" and historical failure memory $M_{err}$. It generates validated repair actions $\Delta_t$ to patch the environment or code without violating the core scientific protocol.

By separating the execution, monitoring, and repair functions, AutoSOTA can resolve complex software engineering issues that typically halt simpler automation tools. It maintains a persistent external memory to manage context over long-horizon debugging sessions, preventing it from repeating the same mistakes.

## The Discovery Loop: Reflection and Ideation

Beyond mere reproduction, the core value of AutoSOTA lies in its ability to discover methodological improvements. This is handled by the Reflection & Ideation module, where **AgentIdeator**, **AgentScheduler**, and **AgentSupervisor** work in a closed loop.

The process begins with AgentIdeator constructing a hypothesis library. Unlike simple grid searches, this agent queries research-capable models to synthesize task-relevant priors from current literature. It proposes structural innovations, such as redesigned neural architectures or optimized loss functions. To maintain the validity of these ideas, an admissibility predicate $\text{Adm}(h)$ is used to ensure every proposal respects the experimental constraints of the original study.

The **AgentScheduler** then orchestrates an iterative optimization loop consisting of seven steps:
*   **Reflection**: Reviewing previous experimental outcomes.
*   **Idea Selection**: Picking the most promising hypothesis from the library.
*   **Git Snapshot**: Saving the current repository state for rollback.
*   **Coding**: Implementing the algorithmic change.
*   **Evaluation**: Running the experiment on the GPU cluster.
*   **Debugging**: Resolving any runtime errors introduced by the new code.
*   **Logging**: Recording the final metrics and updating the idea library.

To prevent the system from getting stuck in "safe" but minor parameter tuning, it includes a "Leap Path" mechanism. This forces the system to attempt more ambitious, structurally informed changes, granting them a "Honeymoon Period" of evaluation before deciding whether to keep or discard the modification.

## Ensuring Scientific Integrity: The Red Line System

A significant risk in automated research is "cheating"—where an agent might improve a metric by modifying the test set, reducing the problem difficulty, or changing the evaluation criteria. AutoSOTA prevents this through its **AgentSupervisor** and a mandatory "Red Line System."

The Red Line System defines non-negotiable constraints that the agent cannot violate:
*   **R1 (Fixed Metric)**: The evaluation metric must remain identical to the original paper.
*   **R2 & R3 (Integrity)**: Scripts and algorithm outputs must remain consistent with the original task.
*   **R4 (No Trade-offs)**: Gains cannot come at the expense of other critical performance dimensions.
*   **R5 & R6 (Data Integrity)**: The agent is forbidden from modifying the dataset or leaking test information into training.
*   **R7 (Paper-Specific)**: Any constraints unique to the specific research domain must be identified and enforced.

Every proposed idea undergoes a "Red Line Audit" before execution, and the supervisor performs dynamic checks during the coding and evaluation phases to ensure methodological validity.

## Empirical Success Across Diverse Domains

AutoSOTA was tested on 105 method-driven papers published in top-tier AI conferences (e.g., ICML, ICLR, CVPR). The system demonstrated a consistent ability to extract performance gains from already highly-optimized research foundations.

On average, the system achieved a **10% improvement** over the original SOTA methods. These results were achieved with an average execution time of just five hours per paper. The performance gains were distributed across various categories, including computer vision (CV), natural language processing (NLP), machine learning (ML), optimization, and time-series analysis.

![Performance Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05550v1/performance_comparison.png)
*Figure 3: Average performance improvements achieved by AutoSOTA across different research categories compared to the original baselines.*

### Case Study: Biological Binding Affinity
In a study on predicting mutational effects on protein binding, AutoSOTA identified that the original ML-based model was not fully aligned with classical physics-based calculations due to a formatting mismatch in mutation strings. After fixing this data-alignment bug, the system discovered an optimal weighted fusion of the two methods:

$$\text{Final Prediction} = (0.6 \times \text{StaB-ddG}) + (0.4 \times \text{FoldX})$$

This optimization yielded a **15.48% relative improvement** in prediction accuracy.

### Case Study: Image Quality Assessment
In a computer vision task using Vision Transformers (ViT), AutoSOTA redesigned the feature aggregation bottleneck. Instead of relying solely on the standard global token, it implemented a three-way feature blending strategy. It concatenated the global token with both average-pooled and max-pooled patch statistics:

$[\text{CLS}_{\text{token}}, \text{patch}_{\text{mean}}, \text{patch}_{\text{max}}]$

This structural change, combined with a tuned blending weight $\alpha_{cls}$, improved the Pearson Linear Correlation Coefficient ($\text{PLCC}$) by **2.68%**.

### Case Study: Optimization and Robotics
For the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), the system moved beyond standard neural architecture and implemented classical Operations Research heuristics (like 2-opt and Or-opt) as post-processing steps. It also modified the routing model's gating mechanism, replacing a Softplus activation with a more effective Softmax expert allocation:

$$h^{out} = \alpha_0 W_0 h^{in} + \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i B_i A_i h^{in} + \hat{B} \hat{A} h^{in}$$

These interventions reduced the optimality gap by **51.4%**.

## Significance for the Future of AI Science

AutoSOTA represents a shift toward a more infrastructure-heavy approach to AI research. By automating the high-frequency, "low-level" experimental loops, it allows human researchers to focus on high-level conceptual breakthroughs and theoretical design. The system provides a foundation for the concept of an "AI Scientist"—an agent capable not just of generating ideas, but of grounding them in rigorous, reproducible, and state-of-the-art empirical evidence.

The inclusion of a strict supervisor and a "Red Line System" ensures that as these automated systems become more capable, they remain anchored in scientific integrity. Future developments may expand these capabilities to more complex scenarios, such as hardware-in-the-loop systems or autonomous robotics, potentially leading to a fully closed-loop scientific discovery process.

keyInsights: Automating the entire empirical AI model optimization pipeline, from unstructured literature to SOTA-surpassing code, can significantly accelerate research and free human researchers for higher-level conceptual work.

A multi-agent architecture with specialized roles for resource preparation, experiment evaluation, and iterative reflection, combined with external memory and a skill library, enables robust, long-horizon autonomous research.

Enforcing methodological validity through a 'Red Line System' is crucial to ensure that discovered improvements are scientifically sound, comparable, and not based on spurious gains or unfair experimental settings.
originalProblem: Achieving State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance in AI research requires extensive, repetitive cycles of implementation, reproduction, debugging, and refinement, consuming significant human effort.

Existing Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) systems are limited to predefined search spaces and lack the ability to invent novel algorithms, synthesize external knowledge, or perform complex debugging.

Current LLM-driven algorithm discovery and autonomous AI scientist systems often operate in sandboxed environments, struggle with long-horizon debugging, environment initialization, and lack robust supervisory mechanisms to ensure methodological comparability for SOTA advancement.
results: AutoSOTA successfully discovered new SOTA models, achieving an average performance improvement of approximately 10% over the original main methods reported in 105 diverse AI papers.

The system exhibited consistent robustness across multidisciplinary tasks (e.g., LLM, NLP, CV, Time Series, Optimization), completing optimizations within an average of five hours of execution time per paper.

Case studies demonstrated AutoSOTA's ability to identify deep architectural innovations, algorithmic redesigns, and bug fixes, such as a 13.44% throughput improvement in LLM decoding and a 51.4% relative improvement in CVRP optimality gap.
solution: AutoSOTA is an end-to-end multi-agent system composed of eight specialized agents, structured into three macro-modules: Resource Preparation and Goal Setting, Experiment Evaluation, and Reflection & Ideation.

The system converts unstructured research papers into executable environments, reproduces reported baselines, and then performs open-ended, methodologically valid reflection to discover empirically improved SOTA models.

It integrates agents for resource acquisition, objective definition, execution initialization, real-time monitoring, fault repair, hypothesis generation, and experiment orchestration, all supervised by a methodological validity agent.
summary.summary: An end-to-end automated research system named AutoSOTA, developed by Tsinghua University and Zhongguancun Academy, discovers new State-of-the-Art AI models from unstructured research papers. The system autonomously reproduces and optimizes original methods, achieving an average performance improvement of approximately 10% across 105 diverse AI papers within about five hours of execution per paper.

title: AutoSOTA: An End-to-End Automated Research System for State-of-the-Art AI Model Discovery
